Key Finding — Negotiation makes the slot date worse, not better
W22–W25 (n=540 11labs, n=806 RBA). Customers who push back end up with appointments 1.4–2.6 days further out.

SMS Overview · W22–W25
When Does the Bot Send an SMS?

SMS Trigger Distribution

Conversations per trigger · W22–W25

Trigger Logic — Same for Both Bots

3 triggers, same in both systems
No common slot found
→
SMS self-service link
After back-and-forth with customer, no availability match
No branch capacity 2+ weeks
→
SMS link to other branch
Sent immediately — sometimes before any negotiation attempt
Appointment cancelled
→
SMS rebook link
Always sent after cancellation so customer can rebook later
On the "3 failed attempts" rule: Not confirmed as a fixed counter. RBA: 0 conversations with 3+ bot misunderstandings. SMS fires when slot negotiation is exhausted — not after a fixed strike count.
Does the Customer Book After Receiving the SMS? (same call)

Post-SMS Booking Rate by Trigger

% of conversations where customer booked within the same call after SMS was sent

Key Insight

Why the rates are so different per trigger

No branch capacity (100% booked same call): The bot sends the SMS but simultaneously offers available slots at a nearby branch. The customer picks one in the same conversation — the SMS link is supplementary, not the primary path.

No slot found & Cancelled (0% booked same call): These are pure take-away SMS — the customer hangs up with the link and is expected to self-serve later. No booking happens in the call itself.

11labs fires SMS earlier (18.9% vs 11.4% for RBA on W22–W25). RBA waits for negotiation to fully exhaust before sending SMS; 11labs sometimes sends in the opening message.

Weekly SMS Rate

W22–W25 — SMS Rate per Week

% of calls where SMS was sent
Conversation IDs — for Product Team Review
No Branch Capacity — SMS in opening message: Expected behaviour for both bots. Difference in frequency: 11labs 8.5% of calls vs RBA 5.6% in W22–W25 — 11labs encounters more branch-capacity situations. No action needed.

⚠️ 11labs — SMS sent without exhausting negotiation (no 3-counter rule)

In these 48 conversations (6.9% of all 11labs calls in W22–W25), 11labs sent an SMS after just 1–8 customer turns — without any fixed attempt counter. The "3 failed attempts" rule does not apply. RBA Parloa: 0 equivalent cases — RBA either continues negotiating or uses the "no slot found" trigger after fully exhausting options. Click any ID to open in ElevenLabs.
What the customer hears: "Entschuldigen Sie die Unannehmlichkeit. Ich habe Ihnen gerade einen Link per SMS gesendet, über den Sie alle verfügbaren Termine einsehen und Ihren Termin bequem selbst verschieben können."
This message fires after 1–8 customer exchanges — with no minimum threshold.

Escalation Overview · W22–W25
Escalation Path — Is One Bot More Frustrating?
✅ Both bots follow the same escalation logic — no major frustration gap
68–72% of escalations are smooth optional offers (SMS sent → "do you also want an agent?"). Both bots have very low customer frustration signals before escalation (<1%). The main difference is that RBA has 8.6% of escalated conversations preceded by a bot misunderstanding vs 0% for 11labs.

Escalation Trigger Types

How escalation was initiated · W22–W25

Turns Before Escalation

Distribution of conversation length before handoff
Escalation Path — Step by Step

11labs Typical Escalation Path

1
Slot offer
→
Bot proposes alternative slot. Customer responds.
2
No match found
→
Slot not available or customer not satisfied. 67.9% of paths.
3
SMS sent
→
Self-service booking link sent in same message.
4
Optional offer
→
"If you'd like, I can also connect you with a colleague." Customer chooses.

RBA Parloa Typical Escalation Path

1
Slot offer
→
Bot proposes alternative slot. Customer responds.
2
No match found
→
After 1–2 rounds of negotiation. 72.2% of paths include SMS first.
3
SMS sent
→
Self-service link sent.
4
Optional offer
→
"I can also connect you with a colleague." 72.2% optional, 8.9% auto.
~
Misund. path
→
8.6% of escalated calls had at least 1 bot misunderstanding before handoff. Slightly more friction.
Frustration & Friction Indicators Before Escalation
Indicator11labsRBAImplication
Avg turns before escalation4.24.8RBA takes slightly longer to reach handoff
Median turns before escalation2411labs escalates earlier in shorter convos
Bot misunderstanding preceded0 (0%)48 (8.6%)RBA has more friction before escalation
Customer frustration language1 (<1%)1 (<1%)Extremely rare — both bots similar
Customer explicitly asked for agent5 (3.6%)14 (2.5%)Both low — escalation is mostly bot-initiated
Smooth path (SMS + optional offer)95 (67.9%)405 (72.2%)RBA slightly smoother on main path
{{waitingTime}} bug3 (0.4%)0 (0%)Production bug in 11labs — wait time not shown
Weekly Escalation Rate

W22–W25 — Escalation Rate per Week

% of calls resulting in escalation · solid = escalation rate · dashed = of which: auto-escalation
Key Differences
✅ Both bots: same escalation logic, smooth path dominant
67–72% of all escalations follow the smooth path: SMS sent → optional offer → customer decides. Neither bot forces escalation automatically (except technical errors).
No structural difference in escalation design
⚠️ RBA: 8.6% of escalated calls had prior bot misunderstanding
48 conversations where the bot failed to understand before escalating. 11labs: 0. This represents extra friction in RBA's escalation path — customer already experienced confusion before handoff.
Review RBA misunderstanding handling — can it escalate sooner in these cases?
11labs escalates 2.5× more often (20.2% vs 8.1%)
On W22–W25: 11labs 20.2% vs RBA 12.7%. 11labs proactively offers escalation in nearly every SMS conversation. This inflates its rate — it's not a sign of more failure, but a more liberal offer strategy.
Confirm if this is intentional — consider aligning the offer threshold

Clara CX Analysis · DE Market · June 2026
How 11labs behaves differently from RBA Parloa
All examples below show specific cases where 11labs takes a different path than RBA — sending SMS earlier, escalating faster, and without applying a fixed attempt counter. 11labs IDs link directly to the ElevenLabs platform.
SMS — 11labs vs RBA

⚠️ 11labs — SMS sent before 3 customer tries (rule violation)

The rule is: try up to 3 times, then send SMS. In these 16 conversations, 11labs sent the SMS after fewer than 3 customer exchanges — breaking the rule. RBA Parloa: 0 equivalent cases. Red badge = sent after just 1–2 cx turns.
16
Breaking rule
SMS before 3 tries
11
At boundary
Exactly 3 tries
21
Following rule
3+ tries before SMS

💡 What if we move to 4 tries?
The 11 boundary conversations (exactly 3 cx turns) are the most likely to benefit from an extra attempt. In several of these, customers were already accepting one of the slots 11labs suggested on their 3rd message — but the SMS was sent anyway:

— "Ja, dann machen wir doch morgen um elf." (Yes, let's do tomorrow at 11) ⭐
— "Ja. Ja." (confirmed agreement) ⭐
— "Nicht um neun Uhr, um eins Uhr" (specific counter-proposal, still engaging) ⭐

These customers were converging on a booking on their 3rd exchange — adding a 4th try would allow the bot to close the deal instead of abandoning to SMS. Given 11labs' overall rescheduling rate of 21.1%, moving from 3 to 4 tries could convert an estimated 2–4 additional successful bookings out of these 11 conversations.
Boundary conversations — 3 cx turns (⭐ = customer was already accepting)

Escalation — 11labs vs RBA

⚡ 11labs — Quick escalation at turn 2 (1.7% of calls · 12 conversations)

11labs escalates to a human agent after just 1 customer exchange — before any slot negotiation has taken place. RBA Parloa: 0 equivalent cases. RBA always attempts slot negotiation before escalating. These are customer-triggered: the customer's first response prompts an immediate handoff.
Common triggers: Customer says "Danke. Bleibe in der Leitung", "Ich möchte Agentin sprechen", or the call is a voicemail/wrong-number detection. RBA never escalates this fast.

Clara CX Analysis · DE Market · W22–W25 · June 2026
What we measure
Days Ahead = Offered Slot Date − Call Date

For each call we extract the first alternative slot proposed, the final confirmed slot, whether SMS was sent and why, and whether escalation occurred and how many turns preceded it.

Step-by-step
1
Get the call date from transaction_time_yyyy_mm_dd.
2
Parse the offered date — spoken ordinals for 11labs ("am dritten Juni"), numeric for RBA ("4. Februar").
3
Classify customer response: accepted / negotiated / abandoned / unclear.
4
Extract final confirmed date from bot confirmation messages.
5
Detect SMS: search for "SMS", "per SMS", "Link geschickt". Classify trigger by context (no slot / no capacity / cancelled).
6
Detect escalation: search for "verbinde Mitarbeiter", "Kollegen", "wartezeit", "{{waitingTime}}". Count turns before escalation, detect bot misunderstandings and customer frustration language preceding it.
Date formats

11labs — Spoken German ordinals

Initial offer
"Ist am dritten Mittwoch Juni, neun Uhr für Sie in Ordnung?"
→ dritten=3, Juni=6 → June 3, 2026
Confirmation
"Ihr Termin ist bestätigt: Dienstag, der dritte Juni"
→ June 3, 2026

RBA Parloa — Numeric dates

Initial offer
"Ist Mittwoch, 4. Februar um 10:30 für Sie in Ordnung?"
→ 4, Februar=2 → Feb 4, 2026
Confirmation
"der Termin ist fest für sie gebucht … am Samstag, 7. Februar"
→ Feb 7, 2026
